prompt:
A CivicWorks Community Services platform capable of operating fully offline provides a unified backend for municipal community operations spanning public content publishing, resident billing, local dispatch, settlement, and analytics. The system defines roles for System Administrator (global configuration and audits), Content Editor (create and schedule public posts), Moderator (comment review and sensitive-word policy), Billing Clerk (fee setup, billing runs, posting and reversals), Dispatcher (zone capacity rules and forced assignments), Driver (accept/decline work with constraints), and Auditor (read-only financial and activity reporting). Content & Resource Management APIs support create/query/update/publish/unpublish for news, policies, events, and classes with category tags, rich-text stored as sanitized HTML, scheduled publishing in local time, and immutable publish history; comments support threaded replies, with pre-save filtering using a configurable local sensitive-word dictionary (exact match plus case-insensitive variants), default “hold for review” if 2+ hits occur, and moderator actions logged. Property Charges & Billing Center APIs support fee-item setup (flat, per-unit, or metered), billing cycles (monthly/quarterly), and automatic A/R generation at 12:05 AM on the cycle date; late fees apply at 5% after a 10-day grace period, capped at $50 per bill, with optional discounts (percentage or fixed) that cannot reduce a bill below $0.00. Driver order-taking and dispatch APIs support grab-order and dispatcher-assigned modes with acceptance constraints (max 3 miles, minimum driver rating 4.2/5.0, and at least 15 minutes online today), hotspot zones with per-zone concurrent capacity, forced dispatch requiring a rejection reason enum and preventing repeat assignment to the same driver for 30 minutes. Payment settlement and reconciliation APIs support offline payment methods (cash, check, voucher, and “other”), full-bill or split/even-split settlement with rounding to the nearest $0.01 and proportional discount allocation, refunds via settlement reversal linked to the original posting, and end-of-day shift handover reports summarizing totals by method with discrepancy workflows when payment totals differ from posted A/R by more than $1.00.

The backend uses Spring Boot to expose resource-scoped APIs with permission boundaries enforced by role-based access control, JPA for transactional persistence, and PostgreSQL for durable local storage in a single-node Docker deployment. Core data models include User (unique username, password hash using BCrypt, role set, status), ContentItem (type enum, title, sanitized_body, tags array, scheduled_at, published_at, state enum, version), Comment (content_id index, parent_id, author_id, moderation_state, filter_hit_count), FeeItem (code unique, calculation_type, rate, taxable_flag), Account (resident identifier, address fields), Bill (account_id, cycle_date, due_date, amount, balance, status), Payment (bill_id, method enum, amount, received_at, shift_id), DispatchOrder (zone_id, status, assigned_driver_id, forced_flag, rejection_reason), and AuditLog (actor_id, action, entity_ref, timestamp). Notification requirements are implemented strictly offline as in-app message records and task reminders (event-triggered and scheduled) with templates, retry counters, and delivery receipts representing read/acknowledged states; email/SMS/IM channels must be configurable but disabled by default and may only write to an outbox table for later manual export, never sending over a network. Search and recommendations are provided via PostgreSQL full-text search with typeahead from recent queries, filters (category, price range, origin), sorting, and per-user search history retention defaulting to 90 days. Non-functional requirements include p95 API latency under 300 ms for typical queries on a single machine with 50 concurrent users, daily local backups to an attached volume, field-level encryption for sensitive resident identifiers, masking in logs, idempotency keys for posting payments and generating bills, and observability via structured application logs plus KPI/report generation stored locally with anomaly flags when arrears increase more than 15% week-over-week.
